name: QUIC Protocol and HTTP/3
description: Advanced quiz with 15 questions on the architecture, multiplexing, handshakes and ecosystem of QUIC.

questions.question: What does the acronym QUIC stand for, and which transport-layer protocol is it built on top of?
options: It stands for Quick URL Internal Connections and operates focused within the IP network.
It stands for Quick UDP Internet Connections and operates directly on top of UDP.
It stands for Quick UDP Internet Connections and operates directly on top of TCP.
It stands for Quick URL Internal Connections and operates focused within web networks.

questions.question: In the initial connection process, what is the great advantage of QUIC compared to TCP with TLS?
options: The format cancels the entire initial handshake and immediately sends the first bit on the network.
The system now requires three slow serial steps to guarantee protection.
QUIC resolves the logical establishment and the encryption in a single handshake.
The protocol shifts the whole weight of the handshake to the efficient edge links.

questions.question: Why did HTTP/2 running over TCP (before QUIC) suffer from the dreaded 'Head-of-Line' (HOL) blocking?
options: Because TCP treats everything as a single continuous stream; one error delays all the rest.
Because the native encryption of the old versions consumed excessive baseline bandwidth.
Because twisted-pair cables ignored the splitting of data originating from the web.
Because the physical network layer queued large requests into a single router.

questions.question: How does the QUIC protocol effectively handle the Head-of-Line (HOL) blocking problem?
options: It consolidates multiple streams into one channel, freezing the queue if any damage occurs.
It forces the definitive exclusion of the whole parallel data right after errors.
It actively hides logical ports, bypassing the application's normal systems.
It isolates all streams; so an isolated error does not block the data of other paths.

questions.question: Where is the QUIC protocol implemented within the traditional layered network model?
options: In the Application layer, running on top of the Transport layer that uses UDP.
In the Transport layer, running directly on top of the pure IP Network protocol.
In the Link layer, shielding the data that comes down from the physical sublayer.
In the Network layer, operating side by side with all native global IP datagrams.

questions.question: Being built on top of a UDP channel (which is unreliable), how does QUIC guarantee data integrity?
options: The protocol ignores bit errors because it focuses entirely on real-time audio.
The model mirrors the robust retransmission algorithms already applied in TCP.
The network interface takes on this complex function as it injects the signals onto the link.
The cloud core locks out malware just before it reaches the access machines.

questions.question: Which statement correctly defines the relationship of the QUIC protocol with Cryptography (such as TLS)?
options: The system dispenses with the adoption of encryption to boost its maximum throughput.
The system's keys are sent only after the exact confirmation of the datagram.
Final security is handed off to the edge routers via dense dedicated tunnels.
The encryption negotiation is unified and done already in the first handshake.

questions.question: Regarding the execution of Congestion Control in the QUIC ecosystem, how does it technically work?
options: Each one of the parallel streams created has its own useful congestion control.
The architecture's control freezes the transfers whenever it detects a congested network.
The system completely turns off this check to gain more fluidity in routing.
The protocol hands the harsh load checking off to the lower layer of the terminal.

questions.question: Where and by which large corporation was the QUIC protocol initially developed and driven?
options: By Microsoft, with its implementation oriented solely to the Windows system.
By Google, with its broad adoption focused on the core servers and on YouTube.
By Mozilla, which aimed to prioritize total security in the new Firefox.
By Apple, with the clear purpose of speeding up all of Safari's links.

questions.question: What is HTTP/3 in the context of the current evolution of the Internet's network architecture?
options: It is an architecture focused purely on exchanging text messages between datacenters.
It is the new protocol that completely excludes the use of encryption to generate more bits.
It is simply the formal definition of the old HTTP/2 encapsulated directly in QUIC.
It is a version focused on closed local physical connections, such as optical fibers.

questions.question: How do browsers guarantee access to old sites that still do not support the QUIC or HTTP/3 protocol?
options: They block the connection because they consider the TCP protocol obsolete and insecure.
They remotely convert the server's code to accept UDP packets.
They require the user to install a specific driver to emulate HTTP/3.
They use TCP as an automatic backward-compatibility (fallback) mechanism.

questions.question: Regarding QUIC's governance and licensing, which statement is correct?
options: It is an open standard (Open Source) developed by Google and now maintained by the IETF.
It is a proprietary, closed protocol, with use restricted to Alphabet Inc. products.
It is paid software (SaaS) that requires annual licensing for network administrators.
It is a technology exclusive to operating systems based on Linux code.

questions.question: Besides Google and YouTube, which companies and services are major adopters of QUIC and HTTP/3 today?
options: Only government defense agencies that use isolated intranet networks.
Large CDNs such as Cloudflare and social networks such as Facebook, Instagram and WhatsApp.
Exclusively email services that operate under the old POP3 protocol.
Only manufacturers of router hardware for use in home networks.

questions.question: Which QUIC feature allows a video on your phone not to be interrupted when you switch from Wi-Fi to 4G?
options: The advanced cookie system that restarts the session in milliseconds.
The data compression that stores the whole video in the cache before the network switch.
Connection ID (CID), which identifies the connection regardless of the IP change.
The native VPN tunnel that masks the device's physical hardware address.

questions.question: What is the main motivation for large content companies (such as Netflix and Meta) to adopt QUIC?
options: To reduce loading latency and improve the experience on unstable networks.
To eliminate the need to use DNS servers for domain translation.
To completely replace the physical optical-fiber layer with radio signals.
To prevent users from using ad blockers in their browsers.
